title: C# Basic — Paper 2
description: C# concepts: strings, nullable types, type casting, operators, conditionals, structs, methods, ref/out, classes, and constructors.

questions.text: What does `string.Format("{0} has {1} items", "Cart", 5)` produce?

options.text: {0} has {1} items
Cart has 5 items
Compilation error
Cart has {1} items

questions.text: Which method removes leading and trailing whitespace from a string in C#?

options.text: string.Strip()
string.Remove()
string.Trim()
string.Clear()

questions.text: What is the result of `"hello world".Split(' ').Length`?
What happens when you use `??=` (null-coalescing assignment) in C#?

options.text: It throws an exception if the left side is null
It assigns the right-hand value to the left-hand variable only if it is currently null
It compares two nullable values
It converts null to false

questions.text: What does `HasValue` property return for a nullable type `int? x = 5;`?

options.text: false
null
true

questions.text: What is the difference between `int? a = null; a.Value` and `a.GetValueOrDefault()`?

options.text: Both return 0 when null
`a.Value` throws InvalidOperationException when null; `GetValueOrDefault()` returns 0
`a.Value` returns null; `GetValueOrDefault()` throws an exception
They behave identically

questions.text: Which Convert method converts a string "42" to an integer in C#?

options.text: Convert.ToString("42")
Convert.ToInt32("42")
Convert.ToDouble("42")
Convert.ToChar("42")

questions.text: What is the result of an implicit conversion from `int` to `double` in C#?

options.text: Data is lost due to truncation
A compile-time error occurs
The conversion is safe; no data loss occurs
A runtime InvalidCastException is thrown

questions.text: What does `int.Parse("3.14")` do in C#?

options.text: Returns 3
Returns 3.14 as int
Throws FormatException
Returns 4 (rounds up)

questions.text: What is the result of `5 << 2` (left shift by 2) in C#?
Which of the following correctly demonstrates short-circuit evaluation in C#?

options.text: In `a & b`, b is always evaluated even if a is false
In `a && b`, b is not evaluated if a is false
In `a | b`, b is never evaluated
In `a || b`, a and b are always evaluated

questions.text: What is the operator precedence outcome of `2 + 3 * 4` in C#?

options.text: B
A
AB
Nothing is printed

questions.text: In C#, which keyword is used in a `switch` statement to prevent fall-through to the next case?

options.text: exit
stop
break
end

questions.text: What does the `default` case in a `switch` statement do?

options.text: It is required and must appear first
It executes when no other case matches
It resets the switch variable to its default value
It throws an exception if reached

questions.text: Which statement about C# structs and classes is correct?

options.text: Classes are value types; structs are reference types
Structs are value types; classes are reference types
Both structs and classes are reference types
Both structs and classes are value types

questions.text: What is one scenario where using a struct is preferred over a class in C#?

options.text: When the type requires inheritance
When the type is large and complex with many methods
When the type is small, immutable, and frequently created (e.g., Point, Color)
When you need polymorphism via virtual methods

questions.text: How do you call a method with an optional parameter `void Print(string msg, bool upper = false)` using only the first argument?

options.text: Print("hello", null);
Print("hello");
Print("hello", undefined);
Print("hello", optional: true);

questions.text: What is the method signature rule for overloading in C#?

options.text: Methods must differ only in return type
Methods must differ in name
Methods must differ in the number or types of parameters
Methods must differ in access modifier

questions.text: What does `params int[] numbers` allow in a C# method?

options.text: Only exactly one integer to be passed
Zero or more integers to be passed as individual arguments
Only arrays to be passed
Only named arguments to be passed

questions.text: Which code correctly uses `out` to return two values from a method?

options.text: void GetValues(out int a, out int b) { a = 1; b = 2; }
void GetValues(ref int a, ref int b) { }
int GetValues(out int a) { return a; }
void GetValues(in int a, in int b) { a = 1; b = 2; }

questions.text: If a method has `ref int x` parameter and you call it with `Method(ref myVar)`, what must be true of `myVar`?

options.text: myVar must be a field, not a local variable
myVar must be initialized before the call
myVar must be declared as static
myVar can be null

questions.text: Can the `in` parameter modifier be used to prevent accidental modification of large structs passed to a method?

options.text: No, in is only for collections
No, in only applies to reference types
Yes, in passes the value by reference but makes it read-only inside the method
Yes, but it copies the struct on every read access

questions.text: What is a constructor in C#?

options.text: A static method that returns a new instance
A special method automatically called when an object is created to initialize it
A method used to destroy an object
An interface that all classes must implement

questions.text: What is constructor chaining using `:this()` in C#?

options.text: Calling the base class constructor from a derived class
One constructor calling another constructor in the same class
A static constructor calling an instance constructor
Calling a constructor via reflection

questions.text: When is a static constructor called in C#?

options.text: Every time a new instance is created
Only when explicitly called in the code
Once, automatically before the first use of the class
At the start of the application, for all classes

questions.text: Which of the following is true about a class with no explicitly defined constructor in C#?

options.text: The code will not compile
A default parameterless constructor is provided by the compiler
Objects cannot be created from that class
All fields are initialized to non-default values

questions.text: What is the correct way to instantiate an object and set properties using object initializer syntax in C#?

options.text: var obj = new MyClass(prop1 = val1, prop2 = val2);
var obj = new MyClass { Prop1 = val1, Prop2 = val2 };
var obj = MyClass.New { Prop1 = val1 };
var obj = create MyClass with { Prop1 = val1 };

questions.text: In C#, what happens to a field in a class that is not explicitly initialized?

options.text: It causes a compile error
It contains random memory garbage
It is automatically set to its type's default value (0, false, null, etc.)
It must be initialized in the constructor or the class won't compile

questions.text: Which keyword is used to refer to the current instance of a class within its own methods?

options.text: base
self
me
this
